postCopy.instagram: Calcutta, August 15th 1947. Indian Independence Day. A British civil servant dead with a partition boundary document that differed from the one about to be announced — a version that would have left fewer people on the wrong side of the line. ✊🔍 Case #149 — Independence Day. Play free at thedailywhodunit.com
postCopy.tiktok: Free daily murder mystery game: Calcutta August 15 1947 Indian Independence Day, a civil servant dead with a partition boundary that would have been different — and whether that difference would have saved the lives it was meant to save. Daily deduction puzzle — thedailywhodunit.com

marketing.imagePrompt: A 1947 Calcutta government office on Indian Independence Day, British colonial furniture and ceiling fans, a British civil servant's briefcase open on the floor with boundary maps and demographic survey documents scattered, an Indian flag visible through the window where crowds celebrate outside, the contrast between the celebration and the stillness of the office. Historical gravity and partition's human cost. Style of Henri Cartier-Bresson's 1947 India photographs.
marketing.animationPrompt: Slow pan through a 1947 Calcutta government office on Independence Day. British colonial furniture. Ceiling fans. A briefcase open on the floor. Boundary maps and demographic surveys scattered. Through the window: an Indian flag and celebrating crowds. Inside: stillness and a dead man. The partition line is about to be announced. This version would have been different. Fade to black.

rookie.title: Independence Day
rookie.setting: Bengal Boundary Commission Office · Calcutta · India · August 15th, 1947
rookie.flavor: British civil servant Arthur Pennington was found dead in the Bengal Boundary Commission office on Indian Independence Day. He had been holding a demographic analysis he had prepared showing that the announced boundary line would leave several hundred thousand Bengali Muslims in India and Bengali Hindus in Pakistan — a reversal from the populations' preferences as documented in his survey. His analysis proposed a boundary adjustment that would have reduced this displacement. The partition line had been announced hours earlier. Three people had been in the office. The analysis was gone.
rookie.emptyState: Independence Day. A boundary adjustment that would have mattered to hundreds of thousands. Gone. Find who took it.

items.name: Commissioner James Hartley
items.detail: The Bengal Boundary Commission's senior British official — had approved the final boundary line and had known Pennington's analysis directly contradicted his decision.

items.name: Advocate Priya Sen
items.detail: An Indian legal advocate who had been representing Bengali Hindu communities in the boundary process — had wanted the boundary where it was and had opposed Pennington's proposed adjustment.

items.name: Clerk Mohammed Hossain
items.detail: A Pakistani-designated civil servant — had been assisting with the partition of records between the two new governments and had known the boundary's effect on his own community.

items.name: The Commission Office
items.detail: Where Pennington was found — briefcase open, boundary maps scattered, analysis missing.

items.name: The Boundary Map Room
items.detail: Adjacent to the main office — where the final boundary documents had been prepared.

items.name: The Building's Rear Entrance
items.detail: The service exit — where a peon had seen someone leaving with documents.

items.name: Survey Map Roller
items.detail: A heavy wooden map roller — blunt force wound consistent with its weighted end.

items.name: Commission Seal Press
items.detail: No wound profile — eliminated.

items.name: Ceiling Fan Pull Chain
items.detail: No wound profile — eliminated.

clues.heading: Pennington's Analysis
clues.text: Pennington's desk notes — found separately from the missing analysis — describe his findings: the announced boundary line placed approximately 230,000 Bengali Muslims in Calcutta's hinterland within India and approximately 180,000 Bengali Hindus in the Khulna district within Pakistan. His demographic survey had shown these populations had expressed preference for the opposite allocation. His proposed adjustment — a boundary shift of approximately fifteen miles in two specific areas — would have reduced this displacement significantly. The adjustment had been submitted to Commissioner Hartley three days before Independence Day and had been rejected.
clues.alone: Hartley had rejected the proposed adjustment three days before Independence Day — the final boundary had already been set. Pennington's analysis showing the human cost of the rejected adjustment was what had been taken.

links.insight: Hartley having rejected the adjustment three days before and Pennington's analysis documenting the rejected adjustment's human cost together establish the specific threat to Hartley — the analysis would have been evidence that a better option had been available and had been rejected.

clues.heading: The Survey Map Roller
clues.text: A heavy wooden map roller — used to store large boundary survey maps — shows blood on its end. Pennington's wound is consistent with the roller used as a bludgeon from behind while he had been at his desk. The roller had been returned to its position in the map room after use. The approach had been from the map room — the door connecting the two rooms had been open.
clues.alone: The roller had been returned to the map room after use — the killer had come from the map room side, through the connecting door. The approach had been deliberate.

links.insight: The killer approaching from the map room and Commissioner Hartley's confirmed presence in the map room that morning together establish the specific spatial position the approach had required.

clues.heading: Priya Sen's Morning
clues.text: Priya Sen had been at the Independence Day ceremony at the Calcutta Maidan from six AM — confirmed by twenty attendees including two judges and a newspaper photographer whose published photograph shows her in the crowd. She had not been in the commission building. She is definitively cleared.
clues.alone: Priya Sen had been at the Independence Day ceremony confirmed by twenty attendees and a published photograph — she had not been in the building. She is definitively cleared.

links.insight: Priya Sen cleared at the ceremony and Mohammed Hossain confirmed at the records division together eliminate both other suspects from the map room — only Hartley's movements between the map room and the main office are unconfirmed.

clues.heading: Hartley's Rejection and Its Basis
clues.text: Commissioner Hartley's rejection of Pennington's adjustment — documented in commission records — had been based on 'the finality of the boundary process and the impracticability of adjustment at this late stage.' The commission's records show the rejection had been made two days before the final boundary had been given to the Viceroy's office — at a point when adjustment had still been technically possible. Hartley had told Pennington in writing: 'The boundary is final. Further analysis of this kind is not helpful.'
clues.alone: Hartley had rejected the adjustment when it had still been technically possible — describing further analysis as 'not helpful.' The rejection had been two days before the boundary had been submitted to the Viceroy.

links.insight: Hartley's rejection when adjustment had still been possible and Pennington's analysis showing the human cost of the rejection together confirm the analysis had documented a decision point that had passed — and had documented Hartley as the person who had closed it.

clues.heading: Hartley in the Map Room
clues.text: A commission peon confirms Commissioner Hartley had been in the map room from approximately eight AM — ostensibly reviewing the boundary documentation for the formal record. The peon had seen Hartley go from the map room toward the connecting door to the main office at approximately nine AM. The killing had occurred between nine and nine-thirty AM. The peon had seen Hartley return to the map room at approximately nine-twenty.
clues.alone: Hartley had gone from the map room through the connecting door at nine AM and had returned at nine-twenty — the killing window was nine to nine-thirty.

links.insight: The approach from the map room and Hartley's nine AM to nine-twenty movement from the map room through the connecting door together bracket the killing window with the specific approach path.

clues.heading: Hartley's Post-Partition Position
clues.text: Commissioner Hartley had been the senior British official responsible for the Bengal boundary. If Pennington's analysis had been received by the new Indian or Pakistani governments, it would have constituted documentary evidence that a better boundary had been available, that a British official had known about it, and had rejected it with the boundary line still adjustable. The analysis would have been damaging to Hartley personally and to the British handling of partition more broadly.
clues.alone: Pennington's analysis would have been damaging to Hartley personally and to the British handling of partition — documentary evidence that a better boundary had been available and had been rejected while adjustment was still possible.

links.insight: Hartley's rejection when adjustment had still been possible and the analysis documenting that rejection together confirm the specific two-part threat — that a better option had existed and that Hartley had closed it.

clues.heading: Mohammed Hossain's Morning
clues.text: Mohammed Hossain had been in the commission's records division on the building's ground floor — separating records for transfer to Pakistan — confirmed by two other clerks and the division supervisor. He had not been on the main office floor. He is definitively cleared.
clues.alone: Hossain had been in the ground-floor records division confirmed by two clerks and the supervisor — he had not been on the main office floor. He is definitively cleared.

links.insight: Priya Sen cleared at the ceremony and Hossain cleared in the records division together eliminate both other suspects — Hartley's map room to main office movement during the killing window is the only confirmed suspect approach consistent with the killing.

clues.heading: Partition and Its Consequences
clues.text: The Partition of India in August 1947 had resulted in one of the largest forced migrations in human history — approximately 10 to 20 million people displaced, and between 200,000 and 2 million deaths in the accompanying violence. The Bengal boundary, announced on August 17th, 1947, had displaced millions of Bengali Hindus and Muslims on both sides. Commissioner Hartley had returned to Britain and had received a CBE for his partition service. Pennington's desk notes are in the British Library's India Office Records. The analysis they describe has never been found.
clues.alone: 10 to 20 million displaced. 200,000 to 2 million deaths. Hartley had received a CBE. Pennington's desk notes are in the British Library. The analysis has never been found.

links.insight: Pennington's analysis showing the human cost of Hartley's rejection and Hartley receiving a CBE together confirm the killing's specific historical context — the analysis had been suppressed, the decision had stood, the consequences had followed, and the decision-maker had been honored.

rookie.epilogue: Commissioner James Hartley had been in the map room from eight AM. He had gone through the connecting door at nine AM. He had used the map roller. He had returned the roller to the map room. He had taken the analysis. He had returned to the map room at nine-twenty. He had returned to Britain. He had received a CBE. The boundary had been announced. The displacement had followed. Pennington's desk notes are in the British Library's India Office Records. The analysis they describe has never been found. Approximately 10 to 20 million people had been displaced by the partition. The Bengal boundary had contributed to the displacement of millions. The analysis that had proposed a fifteen-mile adjustment in two specific areas has not been found.

detective.title: Independence Day
detective.setting: Bengal Boundary Commission Office · Calcutta · India · August 15th, 1947
detective.flavor: Hartley killed Pennington and took the analysis. But Mohammed Hossain had read the analysis before it had been taken — find what he had done with what he had read.
detective.emptyState: Hossain had read Pennington's analysis. Find what he did with that knowledge.

items.name: Commissioner James Hartley
items.detail: The killer — map room approach, roller replaced, analysis taken, CBE, Pennington's notes in British Library.

items.name: Advocate Priya Sen
items.detail: Cleared as killer — Independence Day ceremony confirmed. Her knowledge of the adjustment is the thread.

items.name: Clerk Mohammed Hossain
items.detail: Cleared as killer — records division confirmed. Had read the analysis and had taken a specific action.

items.name: Hossain's Account and Its Preservation
items.detail: What Hossain had done with what he had read — and where it had eventually been preserved.

items.name: The Commission Office
items.detail: Primary crime scene — Hartley's killing.

items.name: The Records Division
items.detail: Hossain's confirmed location — and where he had read the analysis before the killing.

items.name: Dhaka
items.detail: Where Hossain had emigrated — and where his account had eventually been given.

items.name: The Bangladesh National Archive
items.detail: Where Hossain's account had been preserved — and its relationship to the British Library records.

items.name: Survey Map Roller
items.detail: Confirmed murder weapon — Hartley's map room approach.

items.name: Commission Seal Press
items.detail: No wound profile — eliminated.

items.name: Ceiling Fan Pull Chain
items.detail: No wound profile — eliminated.

items.name: Hossain's Memory Account
items.detail: Not a weapon — what Hossain had remembered from reading the analysis and what he had done with it.

items.name: Suppress the Analysis Documenting His Rejected Better Boundary
items.detail: Hartley's motive — CBE, notes in British Library, analysis not found.

items.name: Preserve the Analysis's Substance Through Memory
items.detail: Hossain's post-killing purpose — what he had read and what he had done.

items.name: What Hossain Had Read in the Analysis
items.detail: The specific content he had retained — and how complete his memory had been.

items.name: When and Where Hossain Had Given His Account
items.detail: The timing and institution — and its relationship to the partition's history.

items.name: The Two Archives' Combined Record
items.detail: Pennington's desk notes in the British Library and Hossain's memory account in the Bangladesh National Archive together.

clues.heading: What Hossain Had Read
clues.text: Mohammed Hossain's oral history account — given to the Bangladesh National Archive's partition history project in 1973: 'Two days before Independence Day, Mr. Pennington had shown me the analysis. He had asked me to check the demographic figures against the survey records. I had read the full document. I had seen the proposed adjustment — fifteen miles in the Khulna district and fifteen miles near Jessore. I had seen the figures for the people who would be displaced either way. I had thought: this is the version that should have been used. Then on Independence Day I heard he was dead and the analysis was gone.'
clues.alone: Hossain had read the full analysis two days before the killing — he had seen the specific boundary adjustments and the displacement figures. He had thought it was the version that should have been used.

links.insight: Hossain reading the full analysis two days before the killing and the killing occurring on Independence Day together confirm Hossain had the complete content in memory before Hartley had taken the document — the memory had preceded the destruction.

clues.heading: What Hossain Had Done
clues.text: Hossain's 1973 account: 'After the killing I was transferred to Pakistan — I was a Muslim clerk and my position went with Pakistan. I went to Dhaka. I carried what I had read in my memory. In 1952 I wrote down what I had read in Mr. Pennington's analysis — the proposed boundary, the population figures, the demographic survey data. I wrote it in Bengali. I gave it to the East Pakistan Archives. I said: this is what should have been done and was not done. The archive accepted it.'
clues.alone: Hossain had written down what he had read from memory in 1952 and had given it to the East Pakistan Archives in Bengali — five years after the killing.

links.insight: Hossain writing the memory account in 1952 and giving it to the East Pakistan Archives and Pennington's desk notes being in the British Library together confirm two archives holding two different surviving records of the same analysis.

clues.heading: The Bangladesh National Archive
clues.text: The East Pakistan Archives had become the Bangladesh National Archive following Bangladesh's independence in 1971. Hossain's 1952 Bengali-language account had been in the archive throughout. It had been identified by a partition historian in 1985 who had connected it to Pennington's British Library desk notes. The historian's analysis had been the first to establish that the two documents together — the desk notes' description of the analysis and Hossain's Bengali memory account of the analysis — provided a reconstruction of what Pennington's destroyed analysis had contained.
clues.alone: Two documents in two archives had been connected in 1985 — the desk notes in London and the memory account in Dhaka together reconstructing the destroyed analysis.

links.insight: The 1985 connection of the two archives and the destroyed analysis reconstructed from them together confirm the specific recovery — the analysis had been destroyed in 1947 and had been partially reconstructed from two archives across two continents thirty-eight years later.

clues.heading: What the Reconstruction Showed
clues.text: The 1985 historian's analysis of the reconstructed content had found: the proposed fifteen-mile adjustment in two areas would have kept approximately 190,000 Bengali Muslims within the areas they had preferred to be in and approximately 160,000 Bengali Hindus within their preferred areas. The adjustment would not have eliminated displacement — the partition itself had made complete accommodation impossible — but would have significantly reduced the specific mismatch Pennington had documented. The historian's assessment: 'The proposed adjustment was technically and demographically sound and had been rejected without adequate justification.'
clues.alone: The proposed adjustment would have reduced the specific mismatch significantly — rejected without adequate justification according to the 1985 historian. Not elimination of displacement but reduction of the documented mismatch.

links.insight: The adjustment assessed as sound and rejected without adequate justification and the partition's consequences together confirm the historical assessment — the better option had existed and had been rejected and suppressed.

clues.heading: Hossain's Later Life
clues.text: Mohammed Hossain had emigrated to East Pakistan and had worked in the government archives service until his retirement in 1968. He had given his 1973 oral history account at age sixty-seven. He had died in Dhaka in 1979. He had described the writing of the memory account in 1952 as 'a duty I owed to what I had read — if I had read something important and had not written it down, I would have failed in my duty as an archivist.'
clues.alone: Hossain had described writing the memory account as an archivist's duty — 'if I had read something important and had not written it down, I would have failed in my duty.' He had died in Dhaka in 1979.

links.insight: Hossain's archivist's duty framing and his memory account in the Bangladesh National Archive together confirm the specific professional ethic that had preserved the reconstruction — the archivist's sense of obligation to what he had read.

clues.heading: The Partition's Human Cost
clues.text: The Partition of India had displaced between 10 and 20 million people — one of the largest forced migrations in human history. Between 200,000 and 2 million people had died in the accompanying violence. The Bengal partition had been particularly acute — the boundary line had divided communities, families, and properties with consequences that had extended through generations. The areas Pennington's adjustment had proposed — Khulna and Jessore — had been among the most violently affected by the partition violence in the August-September 1947 period.
clues.alone: The Khulna and Jessore areas — where Pennington's proposed adjustment had applied — had been among the most violently affected in the post-partition period. Between 10 and 20 million displaced. 200,000 to 2 million dead.

links.insight: The most violently affected areas being specifically the areas of Pennington's proposed adjustment and the adjustment assessed as sound and rejected together confirm the historical weight of the rejected option — the analysis had proposed adjustment in the areas where the violence had been worst.

clues.heading: Hartley's CBE and the Historical Record
clues.text: Commissioner James Hartley had returned to Britain and had received a CBE for his partition service in 1948. He had died in 1963. The 1985 historian's analysis had named him as the official who had rejected Pennington's proposed adjustment. The British Library's India Office Records contain Pennington's desk notes. The Bangladesh National Archive contains Hossain's 1952 memory account. Hartley's own commission records are in the British National Archive. Three archives in two countries hold three records of what had happened. Hartley's name is in all three.
clues.alone: Three archives in two countries hold three records — all naming Hartley. He had received a CBE in 1948 and had died in 1963 without seeing the 1985 historical assessment.

links.insight: Hartley named in three archives and the 1985 historical assessment finding the rejection without adequate justification together confirm the posthumous accountability — the historical record had named him and had assessed the rejection after his death, as in the catalog's other cases.

clues.heading: Independence Day and Its Archive
clues.text: August 15th is celebrated as Indian Independence Day. Calcutta — now Kolkata — is the capital of West Bengal, India. The Bangladesh National Archive in Dhaka holds Hossain's 1952 Bengali account. The British Library holds Pennington's desk notes. The areas Pennington had proposed adjusting — Khulna and Jessore — are in Bangladesh. Arthur Pennington is not named in any memorial of the partition period. His desk notes are in the British Library under his name. The analysis they describe has not been found. The partition's consequences are still felt. August 15th is celebrated with fireworks. Both are true.
clues.alone: August 15th is celebrated with fireworks. Pennington is not in any memorial. His desk notes are in the British Library. The analysis has not been found. The partition's consequences are still felt. Both are true.

links.insight: Pennington not in any memorial and the partition's consequences still felt together confirm the final image — the Independence Day that is celebrated annually and the consequences that are still present are not separated by time. Both are true on August 15th.

solution.motive: Suppress the Analysis Documenting His Rejected Better Boundary

detective.epilogue: Hossain had read the analysis two days before the killing. He had gone to Dhaka. He had written down what he had read from memory in 1952 and had given it to the East Pakistan Archives in Bengali — an archivist's duty. A 1985 historian had connected the Dhaka account to the British Library desk notes and had reconstructed what the destroyed analysis had contained. The proposed adjustment had been assessed as technically and demographically sound and rejected without adequate justification. The areas it had proposed adjusting — Khulna and Jessore — had been among the most violently affected in the post-partition period. Hartley had received a CBE in 1948 and had died in 1963. Three archives in two countries. Pennington is not in any memorial. August 15th is celebrated with fireworks. The partition's consequences are still felt. Both are true.

inspector.title: Independence Day
inspector.setting: Bengal Boundary Commission Office · Calcutta · India · August 15th, 1947
inspector.flavor: Hartley killed Pennington. Hossain's memory account reconstructed the analysis. But Priya Sen had also seen the proposed adjustment — and had opposed it. Find what she had done with her opposition and what it meant for her understanding of the killing.
inspector.emptyState: Priya Sen opposed the adjustment and later understood who had killed Pennington. Find what she did with both.

items.detail: The specific basis for her opposition — and whether she had remained in opposition after the killing.

items.detail: She had opposed the adjustment and the killer had suppressed it — the convergence of opposite interests.

items.name: Rejected the Adjustment When It Had Still Been Technically Possible

items.name: Prepared the Analysis That Had Been Both Rejected and Killed For
items.detail: Pennington's foundational role — the analysis, its suppression, and the desk notes that had survived.

clues.heading: Sen's Opposition to the Adjustment
clues.text: Priya Sen's account — given to a Calcutta partition history project in 1980: 'I had opposed Pennington's proposed adjustment. I represented Bengali Hindu communities who had wanted the boundary where it was — my clients' villages were on the Indian side of the proposed line and would have moved to the other side under the adjustment. I had filed a formal objection with the commission. When I heard that Pennington was dead and the analysis was gone, I understood immediately what had happened and who had done it. And I understood something else: that the person who had killed him had the same immediate outcome I had wanted, and for entirely different reasons.'
clues.alone: Sen had opposed the adjustment because her clients would have been moved to the Pakistani side — she had filed a formal objection. When the killing occurred she had understood who had done it, and had understood that Hartley's killing had produced the same outcome she had sought, for entirely different reasons.

links.insight: Sen's formal objection to the adjustment and her understanding that Hartley's killing had produced the outcome she had sought together confirm the specific moral complexity — the advocate had been in formal opposition to the analysis that had been killed, with opposite motivations from the killer.

clues.heading: Sen's Moral Reckoning
clues.text: Sen's 1980 account: 'I have thought about this for thirty-three years. I wanted the boundary where it was. So did Hartley, for different reasons. Hartley killed Pennington to suppress the analysis. I had filed legal papers to suppress the adjustment. We had used different means to the same end. I have asked myself whether my formal opposition had made it easier for Hartley to act — whether the existence of legal opposition had given him a cover for claiming the adjustment had been controversial. I have not resolved this.'
clues.alone: Sen's 1980 moral reckoning — she had used legal means and Hartley had used violence to the same immediate end. She had not resolved whether her opposition had given him cover.

links.insight: Sen's unresolved moral reckoning and Drummond's 1971 unresolved uncertainty together confirm the catalog's recurring pattern — people who had been part of the chain but not the killer, carrying unresolved questions about their own role to the end of their lives.

clues.heading: What Sen Did After the Killing
clues.text: Sen's 1980 account: 'After the killing I did something I had not expected to do. I withdrew my formal objection to the adjustment. The objection had been filed. The boundary had been announced. The withdrawal was procedurally meaningless — the boundary could not be changed. But I withdrew it because I did not want my name to be the legal basis for the outcome that had been produced by a killing. The withdrawal is in the commission records. The boundary did not change. My withdrawal did not change it. But it is in the record.'
clues.alone: Sen had withdrawn her formal objection after the killing — procedurally meaningless but morally significant. The withdrawal is in the commission records alongside the objection.

links.insight: Sen withdrawing the objection and the withdrawal being procedurally meaningless but in the record together confirm the specific moral gesture — an act that had changed nothing in the physical world and had been placed in the record as a statement of position.

clues.heading: The Four Archives and Four Actors
clues.text: Four archives hold four different perspectives on what had happened on and around August 15th, 1947: The British Library: Pennington's desk notes (the analysis described). The Bangladesh National Archive: Hossain's 1952 Bengali memory account (the analysis reconstructed). The British National Archive: Hartley's commission records (the rejection and the official boundary). The West Bengal State Archive: Sen's 1980 account including her formal objection, its withdrawal, and her moral reckoning. Four archives. Four actors. The analysis itself in none of them.
clues.alone: Four archives, four actors, four perspectives — the analysis in none of them. The reconstruction from two archives. The moral reckoning in a third. The official record in a fourth.

links.insight: Four archives and the analysis in none of them together confirm the final archival accounting — the document that had been killed for has been reconstructed from two of the four archives and has not been found in any of them.

clues.heading: The Partition's Living Memory
clues.text: The 1947 Partition of India is within living memory for some — those born in the 1930s and 1940s who had experienced it as children or young adults. Their accounts have been collected by oral history projects in India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh. The partition's consequences — the division of communities, the displacement, the violence — have been subjects of historical, literary, and political examination for over seventy-five years. The Khulna and Jessore areas — where Pennington's proposed adjustment had applied — are in Bangladesh and have continued to have complex histories related to religious and ethnic demographics.
clues.alone: The partition is within living memory. Its consequences have been examined for over seventy-five years. The areas of Pennington's proposed adjustment are in Bangladesh with complex continuing histories.

links.insight: The partition within living memory and its consequences still present together confirm the case's temporal dimension — the killing in 1947 had been at the beginning of consequences that had extended for generations and had not yet fully ended.

clues.heading: Sen's Later Career and Death
clues.text: Priya Sen had continued as an advocate in Calcutta through the 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s. She had been a significant figure in the Calcutta legal community. She had given her 1980 account at age sixty-eight. She had died in Calcutta in 1987. Her withdrawal of the formal objection is in the commission records. Her 1980 oral history account is in the West Bengal State Archive. She had described the withdrawal as 'the only thing I could do that was in my power and that meant something to me, even though it meant nothing to anyone else.'
clues.alone: Sen had died in Calcutta in 1987. Her withdrawal is in the commission records. Her account is in the West Bengal archive. The withdrawal had 'meant something to me, even though it meant nothing to anyone else.'

links.insight: Sen's description of the withdrawal and Hossain's archivist's duty framing together confirm the two moral frameworks that had produced the two surviving accounts — the duty to record and the need for personal moral clarity, both producing documents that had contributed to the historical record.

clues.heading: The Boundary and Its Permanence
clues.text: The Bengal boundary announced on August 17th, 1947 has remained the boundary between India and Bangladesh since Bangladesh's independence in 1971. The boundary's specific line — including the areas Pennington had proposed adjusting — has been a permanent feature of South Asian political geography for over seventy-five years. The adjustment that Pennington had proposed and that Hartley had rejected and killed to suppress has not been implemented. The boundary has been the boundary since August 17th, 1947.
clues.alone: The boundary has been the boundary since August 17th, 1947 — over seventy-five years. The adjustment has not been implemented. The line is permanent.

links.insight: The boundary permanent and the analysis not found together confirm the final accounting — the killing had suppressed the document and the boundary had remained as the killing had been intended to preserve it.

clues.heading: August 15th and Its Archives
clues.text: August 15th is Indian Independence Day, celebrated annually with fireworks, flags, and ceremony. The Bangladesh National Archive in Dhaka holds Hossain's 1952 Bengali account. The British Library holds Pennington's desk notes. The British National Archive holds Hartley's commission records. The West Bengal State Archive holds Sen's 1980 account. Arthur Pennington is not named in any memorial. The boundary has been the boundary since August 17th, 1947. August 15th is celebrated with fireworks. The partition's consequences are still felt. Both are true. The four archives hold the record of what was attempted and what was suppressed. The boundary holds the rest.
clues.alone: Four archives. August 15th with fireworks. The partition's consequences still felt. The boundary holds the rest. Both are true. Pennington is not in any memorial.

links.insight: The boundary permanent and the fireworks on August 15th together confirm the final image — the celebration of independence and the permanence of the consequences are not separated. Both are true on August 15th. The four archives hold what was attempted. The boundary holds what was not.

solution.motive: Suppress the Analysis Documenting His Rejected Better Boundary
solution.accomplice: Written the Memory Account and Given It to the Archive

inspector.epilogue: Priya Sen had opposed the adjustment for her clients and had filed a formal objection. When Pennington was killed she had understood who had done it and had understood the moral complexity of her position — she and Hartley had sought the same immediate outcome by different means. She had withdrawn her formal objection after the killing — procedurally meaningless, morally significant, in the record. She had died in Calcutta in 1987 without resolving whether her opposition had given Hartley cover. Four archives in two countries hold four perspectives. The analysis in none of them. The boundary has been the boundary since August 17th, 1947. August 15th is celebrated with fireworks. The partition's consequences are still felt. Pennington is not in any memorial. Both are true.
